Name: curriculum_via_self_generated_goals
Title: Open-Ended Curricula from Self-Generated Goals
Short Hypothesis: An agent that proposes and filters its own goals using a learned interestingness model will sustain open-ended skill growth longer than fixed-curriculum baselines.
Related Work: Builds on POET, OMNI, and Voyager, which show that environment/goal generation drives open-ended progress, but typically rely on hand-specified novelty signals.
Abstract: We propose a self-curricular agent that generates candidate goals, scores them with a learned model of human notions of interestingness, and trains on the most promising frontier. We hypothesize this yields broader skill coverage and slower plateauing than static curricula.
Experiments: Compare against POET-style minimal criterion coevolution and a fixed curriculum in a procedurally generated benchmark; measure skill coverage, novelty over time, and transfer.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Interestingness models can collapse to trivial novelty; compute cost of goal generation may dominate; benchmark may not capture true open-endedness.

Name: llm_judge_consensus_drift
Title: Measuring Consensus Drift in Open-Ended Research with LLM Judges
Short Hypothesis: Aggregating many LLM judgments over a research corpus reveals stable 'consensus directions' whose drift predicts where a field will move next.
Related Work: Connects automated reviewing and idea-generation systems (e.g., AI Scientist) with quality-diversity notions of measuring a search frontier.
Abstract: We treat a curated literature list as an evolving population and use ensembles of LLM judges to estimate consensus themes and their year-over-year drift, then evaluate whether drift forecasts the emergence of new subfields.
Experiments: Backtest on historical snapshots of an awesome-list; correlate predicted drift with subsequently added entries; ablate judge ensemble size.
Risk Factors and Limitations: LLM judges share biases that can manufacture false consensus; historical snapshots are sparse; correlation need not imply predictive power.
